docs.title: mplbusdayaxis
docs.text: A matplotlib scale for business days that skips weekends and holidays.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install mplbusdayaxis

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import mplbusdayaxis as bda
import numpy as np

dates = pd.date_range("2024-01-01", periods=252, freq="D")
prices = 100 + np.cumsum(np.random.randn(252) * 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(dates, prices)
ax.set_xscale("busday")
plt.show()

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Business Day Scale: X-axis scales based on business days only, skipping weekends
  • Holiday Support: Specify custom holidays to exclude from the scale
  • Compatible with Matplotlib: Works seamlessly with matplotlib's date handling

docs.title: __all__ module-attribute
docs.text: __all__ = ["BusdayScale", "BusdayLocator", "register_scale"]

docs.title: BusdayLocator
docs.text:
BusdayLocator(base_locator=None)

Bases: mticker.Locator

Wrap an arbitrary datetime locator and remove ticks that fall on non-business days.

Parameters:

  • base_locator (matplotlib.ticker.Locator, default: None ) –

    Any datetime-based locator (e.g. AutoDateLocator)

  • busday_kwargs –

    Passed to np.is_busday (e.g. weekmask, holidays)

Source code in src/mplbusdayaxis/_locator.py 
def __init__(self, base_locator=None):
    if base_locator is None:
        base_locator = mticker.MultipleLocator(1)
    self.base_locator = base_locator

docs.title: base_locator instance-attribute
docs.text: base_locator = base_locator

docs.title: set_axis
docs.text:
set_axis(axis)
Source code in src/mplbusdayaxis/_locator.py 
def set_axis(self, axis):
    super().set_axis(axis)
    self.base_locator.set_axis(axis)

docs.title: __call__
docs.text:
__call__()
Source code in src/mplbusdayaxis/_locator.py 
def __call__(self):
    ticks = self.base_locator()

    if len(ticks) == 0:
        return ticks

    busday_kwargs = getattr(self.axis, "_busday_kwargs", {})

    dts = mdates.num2date(ticks)
    days = np.array([np.datetime64(dt.replace(tzinfo=None), "D") for dt in dts])
    mask = np.is_busday(days, **busday_kwargs)
    return np.asarray(ticks)[mask]

def get_transform(self):
    return BusdayTransform(**self._busday_kwargs)

docs.title: set_default_locators_and_formatters
docs.text:
set_default_locators_and_formatters(axis)
Source code in src/mplbusdayaxis/_scale.py 
def set_default_locators_and_formatters(self, axis):
    axis._busday_kwargs = self._busday_kwargs.copy()
    majloc = BusdayLocator()
    majloc.set_axis(axis)
    majfmt = mdates.DateFormatter("%Y-%m-%d")
    axis.set_major_locator(majloc)
    axis.set_major_formatter(majfmt)

docs.title: Business Day Scale Example
docs.text:
This example demonstrates how to use the business day scale in matplotlib.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

import mplbusdayaxis as bda

dates = pd.date_range("2026-01-01", periods=25, freq="D")
values = range(len(dates))

Linear Scale Example

fig, ax = plt.subplots()

ax.plot(dates, values)
ax.set_xscale("linear")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=90)

plt.show()

Business Day Scale Example

Business Day Scale Example with holidays

ax.set_xscale(bda.BusdayScale(ax, holidays=["2026-01-01", "2026-01-08"]))
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=90)
plt.show()

ax.plot(dates, values)
ax.set_xscale("busday", holidays=["2026-01-01", "2026-01-08"])

docs.title: Hourly random walk with zero weekend returns.
docs.text:
Note

Click here to download the full example code

Hourly random walk with zero weekend returns.

Calendar axis: - Daily ticks - Weekend shading - Flat weekend segments

Business axis: - Weekends compressed

Out:

/Users/simonniederberger/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Git/mplbusdayaxis/examples/plot_stock_volume.py:71: UserWarning: FigureCanvasAgg is non-interactive, and thus cannot be shown
  plt.show()


import matplotlib.dates as mdates
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

import mplbusdayaxis

mplbusdayaxis.register_scale()

num_days = 20
dates = pd.date_range("2025-01-01", periods=num_days * 24, freq="h")

returns = np.random.normal(0, 0.002, len(dates))
returns[dates.weekday >= 5] = 0.0

prices = 100 * (1 + pd.Series(returns, index=dates)).cumprod()


fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(8, 7), sharey=True)

fig.suptitle(
    "Comparison of scale='linear' vs scale='busday'", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold"
)


# --- Calendar axis ---
ax1.plot(dates, prices.values, linewidth=1.3)
ax1.set_title("Calendar Time (scale='linear')")
ax1.set_ylabel("Price")

ax1.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.DayLocator())
ax1.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%Y-%m-%d"))
ax1.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=90)

# Shade weekends
full_days = pd.date_range(dates.min().normalize(), dates.max().normalize(), freq="D")

for d in full_days:
    if d.weekday() == 5:  # Saturday
        ax1.axvspan(d, d + pd.Timedelta(days=2), color="grey", alpha=0.15, linewidth=0)


# --- Business axis ---
ax2.plot(dates, prices.values, linewidth=1.3)
ax2.set_xscale("busday")
ax2.set_title("Business Time (scale='busday')")
ax2.set_ylabel("Price")
ax2.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=90)

# Mark weekend boundaries
for d in full_days:
    if d.weekday() == 5:
        ax2.axvline(d, linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.6)
        ax2.axvline(d + pd.Timedelta(days=2), linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.6)


plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96])
plt.show()

Total running time of the script: ( 0 minutes 0.730 seconds)
